title: Analysis of Eigenvalues and Determinants in Political Influence Matrices

The trace of a matrix is the sum of its eigenvalues. Given that the trace of the product matrix $ M_{AB} \times M_{BC} \times M_{CA} $ is 12, and that $ \lambda_1 = 3\lambda_2 $, we can set up the following equations:

$$
\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 12
$$
$$
\lambda_1 = 3\lambda_2
$$

Substituting the second equation into the first:

$$
3\lambda_2 + \lambda_2 = 12
$$
$$
4\lambda_2 = 12
$$
$$
\lambda_2 = 3
$$

Now, using $ \lambda_1 = 3\lambda_2 $:

$$
\lambda_1 = 3 \times 3 = 9
$$

Thus, the eigenvalues are $ \lambda_1 = 9 $ and $ \lambda_2 = 3 $.

Next, if the determinant of the product matrix $ M_{AB} \times M_{BC} \times M_{CA} $ is equal to 36, and the matrices $ M_{AB} $, $ M_{BC} $, and $ M_{CA} $ are all equal, then we can denote each matrix as $ M $. The product matrix becomes $ M \times M \times M = M^3 $.

The determinant of the product of matrices is the product of their determinants. Hence:

$$
\det(M^3) = (\det(M))^3 = 36
$$

To find $ \det(M) $, solve for $ x $ in:

$$
x^3 = 36
$$
$$
x = \sqrt[3]{36}
$$

Since the determinant can be real or complex, the possible values for $ \det(M) $ are the cube roots of 36. The real cube root is $ \sqrt[3]{36} $, and the other two are complex roots given by:

$$
\sqrt[3]{36} \cdot e^{2\pi i / 3} \quad \text{and} \quad \sqrt[3]{36} \cdot e^{4\pi i / 3}
$$

Therefore
